content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I hate the "hero keeps getting up despite sustaining more and more injuries" trope. Which action/thriller films don't have that?

**Score:** 54
**Body:** Nothing takes me out of a movie faster that characters continuing to function after numerous injuries. It's such a dumb trope. What are some movies that don't have this issue, or actually subvert it back to reality, where injuries actually matter? Any year, any country is fine.

Edit: as some comments mention that "the hero keeps on fighting" is sort of the point of the action hero archetype and the genre, yeah, I get that. However, that can be done with at least a modicum of realism. I can't stand movies were people fight on and on while they keep getting shot and hit over the head dozens of times. How many times are you telling me a person can shake off bleeding injuries and blunt force trauma?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Some Jeremy Saulnier films are good for this - Blue Ruin & Green Room are painfully realistic with violence & injuries.
  - I was gonna say Blue Ruin. Subverts so many weary tropes. Fantastic movie.
2. Children of Men. The violence is unexpected, swift and brutal. It brought back the shock and horror of bloodshed, cruelty, torture and death.
3. Edge of Tomorrow
4. The first Mad Max films are like this, he's still carrying a leg injury from the first film in the Road Warrior, and he get's badly injured later in the film.
5. Complete opposite. Love Die Hard
6. John Wick 1: needs to see a doctor to get patched up from time to time

John Wick 4: falls out of a building from 4 stories up, gets up, dusts himself off no problem
7. Bridget Jones Diary has the most realistic fight scene ever IMHO. Two middle aged blokes slugging it out looking like they’ve never had a fight in their life.
8. Steven Segal doesn't take so much as a punch in his first 5 action movies.
9. Uh Reservoir Dogs?

## 2. Movies with discovering super powers; like Split, Unbreakable, or Glass

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I really liked the Universe of the above movies where these characters kinda discover they have superpowers and then they are coming to terms with that. Looking back through their life realizing there was always something off, they weren’t like others, etc.

Maybe even some low key superhero movies, but not the really flashy big ones.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chronicle (2012)

Project Almanac (2015)
  - Chronicle is so good for a raw and real look at “gaining superpowers”. That movie deserves much more praise.
  - Chronicle for sure
2. [Jumper (2008)](https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/jumper). It's campy as hell and not something I would consider great cinema but it's a really fun, bad movie which means it's enjoyable to watch.
3. Brightburn
  - This movie infuriates me.   It really felt like they forgot to film a bunch of the script.  There are just gaps missing in this movie.
4. Rookie of the year
  - Funky Butt lovin
5. Push (2009)
6. Chronicle

## 3. Modern Sci-Fi/Space Films

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I’ve been binging sci-fi movies and have gotten through the Martian, BR2049, Arrival, Prometheus, Dune, Alien Covenant, Interstellar, Life, Ad Astra, Passengers, Gattaca and Gravity.

What should I watch next? Ideally 2010ish onwards but anything goes.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sunshine (2007)
2. Prospect (2018)
3. Doesn’t quite make the cut-off, *trust me* it’s worth it.

Sunshine (2007)
4. Event horizon

Pandorum
5. Start with The Fifth Element (1997), you won't regret it.
6. check out the edge of tomorrow
7. Mickey 17 

Ash

Space Sweepers
8. Outland (1981)

Silent Running (1972)
9. Solaris, Sunshine, Annihilation, Predestination, Source Code, Deja Vu, Ready Player One, Super 8, Project Almanac, Edge of Tomorrow, The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, The Endless, Coherence, Minority Report.
10. Pandorum

## 4. movies about lonely isolated people, that feel alienated from humanity

**Score:** 15
**Body:** title !! i want to watch a film about someone who spends most of their days alone, isn’t mentally super well/normal. They don’t  have proper friends and feel super alien and strange compared to everyone else they meet. Like they almost feel like a different species and also have a quite intricate inner life. movies that sort of feel like this to me are taxi driver, american psycho, the secretary, perks of being a wallflower (if charlie never made any friends lol)… (and yes im describing my life)

edit - thanks sm everyone !! but there’s a lot of wholesome films where the character has a support circle or friends that want the character to be better/understand them etc…. i’m looking for films where everyone finds this character weird and kinda creepy almost, and is avoided by most people, and it’s kinda misunderstood 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Castaway lol
2. You need the double feature of Lost In Translation, and Her.
  - I loved Lost in Translation
3. God's Own Country, about an isolated sheep farmer in Yorkshire. There is eventually a romance, but it's one of those ones where both partners are still somewhat lonely.
4. The Banshees of Inisherin
5. I think you are describing Taxi Driver
6. Moon (2009)
7. The Elephant Man (1980)
8. Donnie Darko
9. **In a lonely place**

Blu-ray Criterion Collection looks terrific. 

When a gifted but washed-up screenwriter with a hair-trigger temper—Humphrey Bogart, in a revelatory, vulnerable performance—becomes the prime suspect in a brutal Tinseltown murder, the only person who can supply an alibi for him is a seductive neighbor (Gloria Grahame) with her own troubled past. The emotionally charged *In a Lonely Place,* freely adapted from a Dorothy B. Hughes thriller, is a brilliant, turbulent mix of suspenseful noir and devastating melodrama, fueled by powerhouse performances. An uncompromising tale of two people desperate to love yet struggling with their demons and each other, this is one of the greatest films of the 1950s, and a benchmark in the career of the classic Hollywood auteur Nicholas Ray.

## 5. Any good war movie recommendations?

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Hey everyone, I’ve recently been on a war movie binge and loved 13 Hours, The Outpost, Land of Bad, and Lone Survivor.
Can you recommend me other similar war movies — realistic, intense, maybe modern-era ones? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Black Hawk Down is fabulous.  Stellar cast and a gripping true story told beautifully.
2. 1917 was fantastic and beautifully shot
3. Warfare. As loud as you can
4. Apocalypse Now 

Platoon

1917

Full Metal Jacket 

All Quiet on the Western Front
  - Apocalypse now is the greatest movies ever made, full metal jacket is always a banger
5. Das Boot is WW2.  German U-Boat movie.  There are 3 versions.   Haven’t watched the longest one.  I think it’s like 5 hrs
6. I mean it's probably not what you're looking for but [Jarhead (2005)](https://www.rottentomatoes.com/m/1152567-jarhead) was pretty true to my experience
7. Paths of Glory
8. The Thin Red Line
9. Platoon (1986)

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What movie caused you to have a "WTF did I just watch?" moment after viewing?

**Score:** 648

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The lighthouse. Still think about it
2. Sorry to Bother You
  - That one turned a sharp corner
  - This is a fun movie to recommend people while telling them they will never, EEEVER, be able to predict where it's gonna go.
3. Tusk… what the hell was that 🤣
4. Beau is afraid
5. Zardoz.

Nothing will ever beat that.  Ever.
6. The Lobster
7. Mulholland Drive
8. I'm thinking of Ending Things

## 2. a good NON-HORROR movie with an autumnal aesthetic? 🍂

**Score:** 524
**Body:** Dead poets society (1989)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Good Will Hunting
  - I just realized Dead Poets is like an emotional prequel to Good Will Hunting
2. When Harry Meet Sally
  - You've Got Mail also fits this I believe
3. October sky
4. School Ties

Rudy 

The Cider House Rules

Fantastic Mr Fox
5. Practical Magic!
6. Knives Out
7. Planes Trains and Automobiles.
8. A Few Good Men is a sneaky fall movie.

## 3. Best monologues of all time in cinema?

**Score:** 518
**Body:** Roy Batty monologue / Blade Runner (1982)

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not exactly a monologue but Unforgiven:

That's right. I've killed women and children. I've killed just about everything that walks or crawled at one time or another. And I'm here to kill you, Little Bill, for what you did to Ned.
2. USS Indianapolis-Jaws
  - Came to say this. Robert Shaw crushed that scene. 
  - “Anyway, we delivered the bahmb.”
3. Stallone's powerful depiction of post-war trauma at the end of "First Blood".
  - I like Trautman's speech:

> "That's gonna look real good on his grave stone in Arlington: Here lies John Rambo, winner of the Congressional Medal of Honor, survivor of countless incursions behind enemy lines. Killed for vagrancy in Jerkwater, USA."
4. That scene in Blade Runner is powerful for many reasons. The monologue, the cinematography, the acting and the music are TOP. It is really touching.
5. I've always thought Roy Betty's monologue is on par with quint from Jaws
6. Orson Welles in The Third Man

“In Italy for thirty years under the Borgias, they had warfare, terror, murder, and bloodshed, but they produced Michelangelo, Leonardo da Vinci, and the Renaissance. In Switzerland, they had brotherly love, they had five hundred years of democracy and peace, and what did that produce? The cuckoo clock.”
7. "Good morning. In less than an hour, aircraft from here will join others from around the world. And you will be launching the largest aerial battle in the history of mankind. "Mankind." That word should have new meaning for all of us today. We can't be consumed by our petty differences anymore. We will be united in our common interests. Perhaps it's fate that today is the Fourth of July, and you will once again be fighting for our freedom... Not from tyranny, oppression, or persecution... but from annihilation. We are fighting for our right to live. To exist. And should we win the day, the Fourth of July will no longer be known as an American holiday, but as the day the world declared in one voice: "We will not go quietly into the night!" We will not vanish without a fight! We're going to live on! We're going to survive! Today we celebrate our Independence Day!"

## 4. The King (2019)

**Score:** 485
**Body:** Good movie, very dialogue focused.  I read that it's based on the Shakespeare plays with plenty of liberty taken so it isn't a strict recreation.  Dramatic and drab, somber and subdued much of the time.  The battles and fights are sparse as this flick shows more of the other sides of medieval life. Personally, I don't like this fictional king. He gets played hard by his own court and doesn't take enough action when needed. 7/10

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He was not a fictional king, Henry V not only existed but he's considered to be the greatest (or one of) warrior king in English history. lmao
2. I loved the realistic sword fights. especially the duel.
  - The way they portrayed fighting in the mud during the battle as well was excellent
  - You mean during medieval times these bros weren’t having beautifully choreographed sword fights lasting ten minutes in 100 pounds of armor with slight visibility while they are fighting to the death?
3. I would have liked it more if the hadn't portrayed Henry as the "reluctant hero" being manipulated by his council, and more of the ambitious young king he was, reigniting the 100 years war using political savvy to justify the war, showing just how close his campaign got to complete disaster in the siege of Harfleur, and how he used his charisma and ability to connect with his men (using the scenes in Shakespeare where he is disguised in camp the night before the battle of Agincourt) and using the storyline of his old drinking buddy to illustrate how he sacrifices his older more down to earth human side to pursue glory....somethin like than. But that opening armored combat scene at the Battle of Shrewsbury was dope...also would have been cool to see Henry eat an arrow during that battle.
  - Ooo, Falstaff’s in this?
4. I understand this movie is more a response to/take on the Shakespeare play rather than a historical fiction per se, but its depiction of Henry V as a naive and easily fooled boy was hard for me to stomach, as someone who knows medieval history.  
  
The real Henry V was 27 when he took the throne. He had been fighting wars and participating in government since the age of 16. He was highly competent in matters of leadership, politics, justice, and waging war. He is widely regarded as one of England's greatest kings. The idea that he got duped into fighting France in 1415 is rather laughable.
  - He used his own bloodline as leverage to extort concessions out of the French. He was very effective in understanding and maneuvering the geopolitics of the era.
5. 9/10 IMO, loved chalamet, Pattinson, and edgerton. The battle was great, the duals were pretty great, score was solid, and the “grimy-ness” of politics and the war made it super appealing. One of Netflix’s best
6. Chalamets performance in this actually sold me as a fan of his acting. Aside from the “make it tissue” speech before the battle of Agincourt, I really enjoyed it.

## 5. Which character do you think was most unfairly treated ?

**Score:** 460
**Body:** Dave from Mystic River

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Andy Dufresne
  - Taking inspiration from you, Edmond Dantès from The Count of Monte Cristo. Based on the book by Alexander Dumb-ass
2. John Coffey
3. Jean Lundegaard from Fargo.

She didn’t even do anything… she just gets kidnapped and murdered because her husband comes up with an insane plan, and unsurprisingly, the thugs that do it are unhinged.
  - Scotty as well.  Loses his mother and grandfather and has to find out his father is the one responsible.
    - Yeah, Scotty. Jeez.
4. The "Oh Crap" guy in Independence Day.

Just minding his own business then gets vaporized by aliens.  Talk about a raw deal.
5. Tom Robinson-To Kill a Mockingbird
6. Truman Burbank

I mean, his entire life was fabricated and filmed for the amusement of others from the moment he was born without ever having any knowledge or choice in the matter. Basically born into a prison where any "free will" he has is just an illusion. I'd say that's a pretty unfair hand to be dealt.
7. Jacob from “Jacob’s Ladder”

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Thoughts on The Smashing Machine [NO SPOILERS]

**Score:** 35
**Body:** Have you ever seen a video game remake and thought... "Why does THIS game need to be remastered?" Well, that's kind of what The Smashing Machine felt like for us.

So my wife and I loved The Iron Claw and were excited to see another wrestling movie by A24. I did some research and saw there was a 90's HBO documentary with the same name about the same guy (Mark Kerr), and it had fantastic reviews! Fun! We can watch it before we go on our movie date.

Well, it ended up being one of the better documentaries I've ever seen. No spoilers, but the amount of raw footage the camera captured got really gave insight of the daily life of a UFC fighter. I was reminded of The Last Dance, another sports documentary that caught an incredible amount of footage of in the 90's that a regular fan wouldn't have seen. I HIGHLY suggest watching the 90's "The Smashing Machine" documentary.

So when we go see the 2025 Smashing Machine in theaters, I'm excited! However I was quickly let down when the movie was basically a frame-for-frame reshoot of that documentary. Within just a few minutes in I was disappointed. I will say, I honestly thought The Rock played the role fantastically in the film. His acting felt effortless.... HOWEVER, he's 52 years old and Mark Kerr was in his 20's when this happened. He looked old, and it makes me wonder why he was a casting choice? Like Zac Effron played The Iron Claw and isn't 52 years old. Super odd. Also how can you have a UFC movie but not show any hard hits? The camera cut away every time.

Also like, there wasn't really much plot to the movie? I mean, there was definitely some character development but again, documentaries don't necessarily "have a plot", it was just a camera crew following Mark Kerr and this new movie just recreates that footage. And the movie really is just a shot-for-shot reshoot of the documentary, but with hollywood actors and some 'larger than life, made for the big screen' moments mixed in.

So again, days later I look back on this movie and it just feels like a true unneeded remake. People who haven't seen the documentary might enjoy it. But I would say to them you're doing yourself a disservice by watching this meh movie over the authentic documentary.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree that it, while being well made, did feel very unnecessary and I was also failing to see the point of turning this into a movie. 

Also quick correction, it’s not a wrestling movie. Mark Kerr was in UFC and had a background in wrestling
2. I don't remember who but I saw a screenwriter tweet that they had almost been hired to write the Smashing Machine, but Johnson vetoed his take on the material because Johnson wanted to remake the documentary beat for beat. So I think it was very much a passion project/vanity project (depending on your view) for the Rock to play a role that means a lot to him. I also agree that the story works much better in the documentary, even though Rock's acting is very good. There's just not enough story for a scripted movie in my opinion.
  - Dwayne produced it too, so he probably had final say creatively.
3. The comparison to the documentary is indeed very apt because the movie is shot in a very documentary style. That also informs the acting - I like your description of Johnson's acting as effortless. From reading reviews, those critics who didn't jive with the movie seem to be out-of-step with Safdie's artistic decision to shoot his movie "around" the actual fighting in the arena. This is really a drama whose relationship to wrestling is almost incidental. No doubt this was done to avoid doing "just another wrestling movie" but it can render parts of the film quite toothless. That's the problem of stepping into such a well-worn genre.
  - That's true but it's also a drama whose relationship to drama is almost incidental. Nothing happens either on or off the ring.
4. I am a mma fan and i know about Mark Kerr since his days at Pride fc, but i didn't know he had a documentary about him, and that Johnson wanted a drama movie based on it.

It was... Decent. Johnson isn't the greatest actor and it shows, he doesn't deliver the pain and anguish Kerr had. I'll watch the documentary because the movie isn't that good.
5. I 100% felt the same way. Im a fan of MMA in general and had seen the documentary…it was a bit egregious how much of a direct recreation it was. 

Felt like an excuse to try to get The Rock nominated and validate his ability to act, which granted it did. 

His performance was great, relative to his previous work, but if a mid level football player turned sprinter sets a personal best time for a 100 m dash, it doesn’t make them deserving of an olympic gold medal. 

[I wrote a review if anyone is interested](https://saysheez.com/blog/the-smashing-machine)
6. Haven’t seen the documentary, but I loved the film. Did the documentary have Mark walking around his cluttered house looking for drugs while Lil Suzy plays in the background? Did it have his wife vibing out on the Gravitron? Did it show their argument about a cactus turn into one about parenthood? I’m definitely interested in catching the doc as well, but it’s these moments more than the fighting that did it for me.
  - Yep, it has all that!
7. I haven't seen the documentary but I thought it was a bore. I've never heard of Mark Kerr but I did grow up with people who were into the UFC so I have a slight familiarity with the sport. 

  
The story just felt so cliche. Like does every fighter live the same life? This is the most The Rock has ever embodied a role but he also has a low bar to clear for a great performance. Emily Blunt is great as well but the character only had one note. 

He goes to rehab and just comes back a scene later all better. There's no build up to the final fights and all of a sudden we're focusing on his friend's final match instead of Kerr. 

Love the Safdies but was disappointed with this one.

## 2. I think the greatest issue with film sequels, or any sequels in novels, comics, etc. is when they negate or diminish the previous film's theme, accomplishments, and character arcs.

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I love a good sequel, especially one that expands of the ideas and mythos.  Great sequels can approach it many ways:

 \- Expanding and deepening the characters and mythos (Aliens, Terminator 2, Godfather 2, Bladerunner 2049, Empire Strikes Back)

\- Just giving us more of the same but maybe a bit more intense (Mad Max The Road Warrior, Prey, Raid 2)

\- Going off in a completely different but fun direction in regards to tone or characters or just made a new film with some of the same elements (Gremlins 2, 10 Cloverfield Lane)

As with all films, most are unsuccessful but I appreciate the attempts. However what actually bothers me and makes me actively dislike a sequel is when they take the characters and their experiences/accomplishments from the previous film and negate them. This includes:

\- Having the characters who spent the previous film fighting to survive and forming a bond die almost immediately after the last film or at the start of the sequel (Alien 3, Terminator Dark Fate, Mortal Combat).

\- The the relationships built immediately fell apart or went to hell after their ending (Karate Kid 2, Star Wars The Force Awakens)

\- The characters we knew and loved who ended the last film in triumph are now sad failures an, (often dying sad and alone in the film) (Beetlejuice 2, Star Wards Force Awakens and its sequels again)

I have taken part in creative writing activities where others start a story and you continue it, and the most hack/disrespectful things you can do is to just dismiss the existing and just say "and then they died." Is there a term for this?

To be clear there are exceptions and I am not saying you cannot have additional drama and elements above. For example Bladerunner 2049, however the end of Bladerunner was hardly triumphant and a dark future was anticipated. However again Alien3, Terminator Dark Fate, and the most recent Star Wars Trilogy left me with a "what's the point" of our hero's journey in the preceding films. Thoughts?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I agree that there are good ways and bad ways to write a sequel, but I reject the idea that bad things happening in the interim of the first and second movie negates the purpose of the previous journey. Something like Alien 3 where they kill all the previous characters offscreen for what is obviously budget reasons and creative bankruptcy is one thing, but in The Force Awakens, a movie I don't particularly like the plot of, they give a clear reason why the relationships fell apart in the "death" of their son/nephew and the trauma it caused.

Even if you ignore the sequel trilogy, it's silly to assume that the end of Return of the Jedi means that all bad things in the galaxy ended or that there wouldn't ever be conflict again. I don't really like the retcons of the sequel trilogy much at all, but if anything, it makes things too pat with how fast the Empire dissolved and such. The sequel trilogy making things bad doesn't negate the happy ending any more than the knowledge that Luke will eventually defeat the Emperor takes away from the tragedy of Revenge of the Sith.
2. I think spectacle creep or power creep can also hamstring a sequel, especially if it iterates through multiple sequels. Always having to top the previous installment can really put writers in a corner. Death Star? Starkiller Base. Imperial-class Star Destroyers? An armada of planet-killer Xyston-class Star Destroyers. Executor-class Star Destroyer? Resurgent-class Star Destroyer. Eventually the scale stops registering, and a thorough descaling is needed to appreciate the spectacle again. 

Not always a bad thing, though. Sometimes you go from stealing VCR/DVD players to car wizards in space
  - Fast & Furious is the perfect example of a movie series that began as a *kind of* grounded story that just got bigger and bigger and more spectacular and more spectacular to a ridiculous degree. You start with a Point Break-ish story about a cop infiltrating illegal street races to literally outer space, as you said.
3. I personally believe that audiences don't know character, narrative, literary, thematic structure enough to know what matches or not. I remember what life was like before the internet and the accidental breadth of knowledge I had to consume to understand my favorite movies more. But on the internet I see a lot of recursive and repeated statements that don't seem to have the level of insight needed to form an objective statement or opinion. The knee jerk reaction is searched for and preferable hashtag defines how they feel moving forward, this sort of hashtag zombism with no other source of information besides other opinions. And what I mean by objective opinion is that we can both agree on objectively measurable devices used in films to then opine on if it was utilized effectively.
  - What is an objectively measurably device in a film?
4. Sequels tend to trivialize a lot of things in the interest of making a new story or trying to find a "bigger bad" but it almost always ends in making the previous entry seem like fairly low stakes. That's why Shrek and Shrek 2 or The Exorcist 1 and 3 are lauded so much, because they retain the relative groundedness of their original narratives and don't try to over embellish the heroes' traits or give them some knowledge or opponent that breaks the rules of their established universe.
5. One thought would be that a successful sequel has to do two opposite things at the same time.

First, it has to repeat/recapture what made the original film successful, what people liked about it. Second, it has to offer something new and can't just be a repeat. 

Conceptually, walking that tightrope can be difficult. I think about movies like Jurassic World and The Force Awakens, which exist at the Venn diagram overlap between remake, reboot and sequel.
  - I don't think there's any real pressure for a sequel to "recapture" anything the original had. Maybe from studios, but I think some of the best sequels were ones that crafted their own premise while staying true to the world's rules from the first.
    - I don't think we disagree.

You talk about "staying true to the world's rules," which is exactly what I mean. Part of why people like something like the original Star Wars is because they like imaginatively spending time in that world, which is something recaptured in the sequels.

If you made a Star Wars movie that was a grounded slice of life drama without spaceships traveling to other planets or galactic warfare or Jedi and Sith, I don't think it would go over well. Same thing if you made a Mad Max sequel that wasn't set in a post-apocalyptic wasteland, etc.
  - The “reboot sequel” is an absolute scourge on Hollywood and needs to be stamped out. The goal of reboot is to “reset” the story. The goal of a sequel is to continue the same story. It’s not that it’s simply a difficult tightrope, these are contradictory goals that cannot be reconciled.

## 3. As this happens for many movies, I've heard that Denzel Washington was first offered the role of Det. David Mills for Se7en.

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Do any of you know if the script was already settled when this happened ? I can't imagine Denzel playing the role of a fragile, unexperienced detective as Brad Pitt did. Of course, Denzel did a great job in a movie like Flight, but I feel like it's something else entirely.

Also, the apex of the movie is arguably the final scene. Denzel Washington is probably my favorite actor all things considered (lot of love for DDL, Gyllenhaal, Farrell and many others, but I could binge watch DW's filmography for days without getting bored), but I can't see him match the heart wrenching acting that Pitt displays at that point. It's just not his style, imho.

Would the film have taken a different direction with a different cast ?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'd add that I think it's one of Pitt's best performance. I used to be a huge fan of him but I later came to realize that I mostly was in love with the films he played in (in the 90s in particular) more than his acting - probably I've watched him too much; so much so that I eventually lost the ability to forget he is Brad Pitt and not his role.  
  
He has a mesmerizing presence (his role in Joe Black is almost photographic); in that way, Robert Pattinson makes me think a lot of him. But in Se7en, he's... wow.
  - Brad Pitt is one of those actors who is so charismatic that it becomes harder and harder to see them as the character they're playing rather than the actor playing a role.

But that's what makes him a movie star rather than just a great character actor. He was *amazing* in Tree of Life for example, but if Daniel Day Lewis played that role I would be thinking how great the character was rather than how great Brad Pitt's performance was as that character...
2. Hes said in interviews he turned it down because it was too evil and he was genuinely disturbed, but of course he regrets it because Seven was a huge hit. I think he'd be pretty great in it actually, even though Pitts performance is so good.
3. A few years after Seven Denzel started in Fallen (1998) and it feels heavily inspired by Seven. I thought Denzel did a great job in it. 

The movie is a little more cheesy than Seven, but man does it swing for the fences. My hot take is that Fallen is a more interesting movie than Seven. 

I think Denzel would’ve been able to handle the heart wrenching ending. He was in Glory a few years earlier and conveyed a lot of emotional pain. 

I don’t think he would’ve been the same type of aloof? as Pitt though.
  - Yeah, I liked Fallen a lot. But it's clearly a different vibe.
4. Pitt was absolutely perfect as Mills. He portrays a mix of cockiness, competence, humour, and momentary rage so well, that Denzel wouldn’t have captured in the same fashion. Mills has the off-the-charts movie star charisma of Pitt, but also has an intensely grounded, human element that makes Mills such a memorable character, compared to others of his archetype of the rookie.

## 4. 'One battle after another ' is a house with many mansions and show us the power of the to-be-determined style of directing

**Score:** 0
**Body:** '*A whole world sprouted out of nowhere*', Leonardo DiCaprio said in a recent interview about the movie One Battle after Another. And yes this is one of the signifiers of a house with many mansions, which the movie can be likened to. Lets prepare ourselves, with a poised inner attitude (ocean waves, ocean waves) and not miss the opportunity for shaking off the chain around the angle, that has kept us locked within a narrowed mindset. For centuries upon centuries. 

Leonardo was referring to some of the scenes with Benicio del Toro, where the creative vision of Paul Thomas Anderson met with the ideas that Benicio brought to the set. Not only Benicio but many others of the lead actors brought significant ideas to the set. The actors were not merely clay in the hands of the director (like a complacent AI tool would be), but were significant cocreative participants, who brought this movie from draft upon draft on paper to the cinema screen. The director PTA was literally waiting twenty years for these guys to show up, and now the movie is here, exerting its influence in the cultural bloodstream. And what a rush.

The TBD (=to be determined) style of directing requires a certain maturity for those involved. It cannot be rushed. Nature do not rush, and still we see how everything works out in a marvellous manner. Worlds are sprouting, despite thelockjawed effort to keep everything "clean". 

A myriad of movies have been made where not only the script, but the unfolding of each scene, was micro-managed from the very beginning. We do not talk about those movies, because like a fart in the wind they do not last very long. 

An immature part of us cling to the predetermined, where the glorification of a certain narrative sets up "certainties". That part of us finds it unsettling - sometimes unbearable - when a movie is unclear about who the good guy or the superhero is. A narrative could be white supremacy, climate safety or any other allegiance towards a common enemy. The socalled certainties are only certainties for as long as the narrative hold sway against the relentless waves in the ocean of the potential. Eventually false certainties fall. Not because of brute force, but because of the subtle influence from within that is born in the field of tension between polar opposites. 

'*Be careful*', Bob says. 

'*I won't*', is the mature reply.  

To rest in the place of not-knowing, to find your sense of security in the place of not knowing , takes trust. 

Lockjaw has put all of his chips in the basket labelled christmas adventurers club. What he perceives as himself will cease to exist if that narrative - as an outset for action where men do what men do - crumbles. To say that this frightens him would be an understatement. He has fallen off the cliff long ago, and when his bloody face re-appears in the horizon, like a resurrected terminator, we might realize the scope of the challenge we are faced with. 

Using the lense of a certain narrative it is immense. And will be one battle after another.

Using the lense of trust it is hilarious and a fart in the wind. 

So which is it? 

Joyful will,

Johan Tino

This movie was clearly incredibly collaborative. It reminds me of Ridley Scott romantically remembering how Alien’s entire look came together from the work of 20 people in his dvd commentary.
